notes: The encoding's source declaration explicitly calls it a transcription and not an edition.

access.accessMethod: The public record lists three bitstreams, but their routes are marked isAllowed=n and present an interactive terms gate. The terms were not accepted in this research run.

rights.basis: The pinned XML says the text was made available through OTA for personal scholarly use only. access.accessMethod: Apply in writing with the subject and purpose of research, provide an academic letter of introduction, and book in advance; confirm current availability before travel.
notes: The current page says manuscript resources are available for study but also notes temporary storage limitations during Wren Library repair work.

access.accessMethod: Request digital images or photocopies from the Cathedral librarian; charges apply and feasibility, resolution, delivery, and written reuse terms must be confirmed.
access.format: Digital images or photocopies on request
notes: For a complete-source request, specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and every manuscript leaf separately from the Octavian work portion ff. 98va-109rb.

rights.basis: The current reader page advertises charged digital images and photocopies but does not publish item-specific reuse terms for a requested MS 91 surrogate.
rights.component: Any manuscript images or photocopies supplied in response to a reproduction request
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
notes: Obtain written terms before placing supplied images in Git, a public viewer, a PDF publication, or another redistribution workflow.

entities.description: Robert Thornton's copy of the Northern Octavian romance in a large paper miscellany. The component begins and ends within shared folio surfaces and is textually incomplete because a leaf is wanting between ff. 102 and 103 and half of f. 108 is torn away.

holding.notes: The catalog component is item 5, Octovyane, ff. 98v-109; DIMEV refines the column span to ff. 98va-109rb.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, Octovian component
notes: The official catalog states that the entire manuscript was written by one scribe, Robert Thornton, and was written by and for him at East Newton, North Yorkshire.
DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME profile 1.98 (Lincolnshire); that linguistic localization is not substituted for the codex's North Yorkshire association.

physicalDescription.extent: 322 leaves in current nineteenth-century numbering, including 133A; original minimum approximately 340 leaves
notes: Two columns from ff. 98v-153v, generally about 36-39 lines.
The Octovian component shares f. 98v with the close of the Alliterative Morte Arthure and f. 109r with the opening of Sir Isumbras.
A leaf is missing between ff. 102 and 103, and half of f. 108 is torn away.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval text and fifteenth-century witness predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Medieval textual content embodied in the fifteenth-century witness
notes: This does not cover modern manuscript photography, catalog prose, facsimile design, or provider contracts.

access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, Octavian component
access.format: Scholarly manuscript description; not a holding-institution catalog record

entities.description: A separate Northern Octavian witness at ff. 90rb-102vb, containing 1,731 lines. It supplies the facing even-page transcription in McSparran 1986 and passages used to repair Lincoln losses in Hudson 2006; it is not the textual source of OctL.

holding.notes: The consulted exact component descriptions were scholarly DMER and DIMEV records; no repository-owned exact online manuscript catalog URL was verified.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, Octavian component

notes: DMER reports two scribes, with the second taking over in the middle of Octavian.

rights.basis: The scholarly edition was published in 1986, is available through controlled lending, and no public-domain dedication or open license for its modern editorial content was verified.

bibliographic.citation: Octovian, ed. Frances McSparran, Early English Text Society, Original Series 289 (London and New York: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1986).
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 289
bibliographic.title: Octovian

entities.description: A parallel-text edition of the Lincoln and Cambridge Northern witnesses. Lincoln appears on odd pages 77-179 and Cambridge on even pages 76-178; the two transcriptions are not homogenized into the direct OctL text.
entities.label: Frances McSparran's Octovian (EETS o.s. 289, 1986)
notes: Lincoln's text occupies odd pages and Cambridge's text even pages.
The Lincoln holding catalog miscites the series as 298; Folger, DIMEV, DMER, and the edition's cataloging identify o.s. 289.

rights.basis: The scholarly edition was published in 1986, is available through controlled lending, and no public-domain dedication or open license for its modern editorial content was verified.

localCopies.label: Complete METS PDF of Hudson, Four Middle English Romances, 2nd edition
notes: pdfinfo confirms 206 pages and pdftotext extracts all pages. qpdf reports recoverable object-offset-zero warnings but completes; the simple file utility misreports eight pages. The provider bytes are retained unchanged rather than silently repaired.

workPortion.label: Octavian introduction, edited text, and notes
notes: Introduction: PDF 40-44 (printed 39-43); blank separator: PDF 45; edited text: PDF 46-89 (printed 45-88); notes: PDF 90-96 (printed 89-95).

notes: Public download is access, not a blanket republication license for the PDF or modern editorial material.

entities.description: A later normalized scholarly reading edition based on the Lincoln witness but supplying missing or badly damaged passages from Cambridge MS Ff.2.38. Its 1,848-line composite extent is not OctL's extent.

The cached source is the complete 206-page anthology PDF, not a crop of Octavian.

next_steps: Ask the Cathedral librarian for availability, quote, resolution, delivery format, completeness, and written reuse terms.
Specify boards or covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every manuscript leaf, and the narrower work portion separately.
Use the 1975/1977 printed facsimile onsite for visual comparison if a digital surrogate is unavailable.
openQuestions.question: Does Lincoln Cathedral hold a complete digital surrogate of MS 91 that can be supplied or licensed, and can it include every physical surface as well as Octavian ff. 98va-109rb?

next_steps: Ask U-M and OTA for the U-1683-A deposit and permission record and the intended scope of the OTA item license, bitstream terms, U-M rights statement, and MEDictionary availability statement.
Keep the medieval text, modern transcription/markup, catalog metadata, printed editions, and manuscript photographs as separate rights components.
Do not rely on the CME software-framed Unlicense to resolve inherited third-party data without clarification.
openQuestions.question: How should OTA's embedded personal-scholarly-use-only notice be reconciled with the current OTA record's CC BY 3.0 metadata, U-M's No Copyright-United States label, and the MEDictionary descendant's broader public-domain assertion for redistribution of the exact transcription and markup?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether historical conversion or parallel-text documentation survives.
Compare the pointers with McSparran's facing-page alignment and any original OTA/SGML package if it becomes available.
Continue treating the values only as observed partial concordance markers, never as line counts or documented companion line numbers.
openQuestions.question: What external target namespace or comparison workflow did the pinned XPTR/@TO values serve, and how were their zero and repeated values intended to be interpreted?

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript facsimile and McSparran's lineation around the final amen and damaged f. 108.
Document whether one resource excludes a final formula, damaged fragment, or another display position before changing any extent.
openQuestions.question: Which counting convention explains DIMEV's 1,628 lines versus DMER's 1,629 and the pinned encoding's 1,629 verbal positions within 1,634 L containers?

next_steps: Obtain the OTA bitstreams through an authorized route with their exact governing terms and request U-M conversion documentation.
Compare exact files byte-for-byte if obtained, keeping wrapper conversion distinct from textual revision.
openQuestions.question: What exact files and transformation steps connect McSparran's working transcription, OTA item 1683/U-1683-A (especially octl-1683.txt), U-M's 1993 DLPSTEXTCLASS serialization, the current MEDictionary ETS/EEBO serialization, and the CME git artifact?

subject.description: Record 3132 witness 3132-2 and manuscript record Lincoln91

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the exact pinned XML artifact; the cataloged intellectual boundary is the Lincoln witness-version, not Northern Octavian in the abstract.

The pinned artifact and current Middle English Compendium descendant retain the same BODY text-node stream after Unicode-alphanumeric normalization, but their wrappers, rights statements, and XPTR serialization differ; they are versions, not asserted byte-identical copies.

The pinned OctL and OctC files each contain 1,400 nonzero XPTR/@TO occurrences and 1,397 unique nonzero values, with an intersection of 1,180. These values show partial concordance behavior, not textual extent; their exact target namespace and intended application remain undocumented.
Line extent is kept as three attributed measurements: 1,634 XML L positions (five empty UNCLEAR positions), 1,629 verbal positions and DMER lines, and DIMEV's 1,628-line report.

No complete public image surrogate for Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 was verified. The one current catalog image is only part of f. 80r and does not overlap Octavian; the ignored cache records that limited access without presenting it as a work facsimile.

The exact current OTA record is public and lists three files, but its bitstream routes are marked isAllowed=n and require interactive terms. Those terms were not accepted, the bitstreams were not downloaded or compared, and the record's CC BY 3.0 metadata is kept separate from content-level rights.

lineage.summary: The pinned repository artifact is a version of University of Michigan CME item OctL, associated with the extant Oxford Text Archive record 1683/U-1683-A. The separately modeled current Middle English Compendium XML is a descendant serialization of the pinned layer, not the U-M encoding itself. The electronic text is Frances McSparran's direct transcription of the Octavian component in Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, not a transcription from her 1986 EETS edition; only EETS odd-page milestones were added for comparison. The Lincoln witness is physically incomplete through a missing leaf after f. 102 and loss of half of f. 108. McSparran's 1986 parallel edition, Hudson's 2006 Lincoln-based composite with Cambridge supplies, DIMEV, and the 1975 printed Thornton facsimile are modeled as distinct comparison, description, or access layers rather than silent upstream sources of OctL's wording.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed c. 1350; Lincoln witness copied c. 1430-1450
catalogSummary.region: Northern version tentatively associated with the Northeast Midlands or Yorkshire; Lincoln copy made by Robert Thornton of East Newton, North Yorkshire; DIMEV reports LALME 1.98 (Lincolnshire) for the manuscript's language
catalogSummary.title: Octavian (Lincoln Thornton manuscript version)

catalogingSubject.label: Octavian (Lincoln Thornton manuscript version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English Octavian romance as witnessed, with physical losses, in Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, ff. 98va-109rb, and represented by Frances McSparran's direct transcription in CME item OctL. This exact witness-version is related to but not interchangeable with the abstract Northern recension, its Cambridge and early-print witnesses, the distinct Southern version, McSparran's 1986 parallel edition, Hudson's later composite reading text, the separate OTA, U-M, and current MEDictionary encoding layers as digital objects, or this repository's XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Lincoln Thornton manuscript version of Octavian

locators.value: ff. 98va-109rb (DIMEV); ff. 98v-109r in the holding catalog
pp. 77-179, odd pages; used in OctL only for comparison milestones

notes: Folio 98v is shared with the end of the Alliterative Morte Arthure, and f. 109r is shared with the opening of Sir Isumbras.
The 1,629-line value is a reproducible verbal-position count, not a silent correction of DIMEV's 1,628-line statement.
contentParts.summary: The direct, unreconstructed Lincoln witness text from its opening invocation through its surviving ending, including gaps caused by a missing leaf after f. 102 and the torn half of f. 108.

assertion.notes: The repository XML gives the broader inherited range 1350-1375; the date remains approximate and is not normalized to an exact interval.

scope.description: The Northern Octavian work represented by the Lincoln witness-version

assertion.notes: This is the Lincoln copy's production range. The consulted sources do not securely establish its chronological priority over the Cambridge Northern witness, also dated broadly to the mid-fifteenth century.

extent.description: One damaged manuscript-version of the romance. The pinned XML has 1,634 uniquely numbered L positions, of which five (1521-1524 and 1544) contain only empty UNCLEAR elements, leaving 1,629 positions with verbal text; DMER likewise reports 1,629 lines, while DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 reports 1,628. The XML also has 17 supplied navigation DIV1 chunks—sixteen 100-position chunks plus a final 34-position chunk—49 EETS page milestones, no LG stanza containers, and no BODY prose paragraphs. The 17 chunks and final line number are representation metrics, not intrinsic sections or proof of complete textual survival.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse, predominantly in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas; the source XML supplies no stanza-group markup, so no stanza count is inferred from line arithmetic.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief formulaic words or phrases such as 'Amen' and 'par charyte' do not make the whole work multilingual.
The cataloging boundary is deliberately witness-specific: statements about the abstract Northern recension are not silently transferred to every Lincoln reading.
Robert Thornton is modeled as scribe and compiler, not author.
The manuscript's missing leaf and torn half-leaf are physical losses; Hudson's Cambridge-supplied reconstruction is not counted as OctL text.
Public facsimile availability, access routes, image terms, digital-provenance uncertainty, and component-specific rights are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's counting convention with McSparran 1986 and the manuscript facsimile, especially the final amen line and damaged f. 108 positions.
Retain all three attributed measurements until the discrepancy is documented.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV report 1,628 lines for the Lincoln witness while the pinned XML has 1,629 nonempty verbal line positions and DMER reports 1,629?

next_steps: Consult McSparran's linguistic analysis and current dialect scholarship.
Model competing composition and scribal-language claims separately if they cannot be reconciled.
openQuestions.question: Can the Lincoln witness-version's composition stage be localized more narrowly than the tentative Northeast Midlands or Yorkshire proposal without conflating work origin, Thornton's North Yorkshire association, and the manuscript's reported Lincolnshire scribal profile?

next_steps: Consult McSparran's source study and later scholarship before identifying a physical or textual French exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which Old French textual branch or exemplar most directly underlies the Northern English version represented by Lincoln MS 91?

assertion.notes: DMER gives 'Northeast Midlands, Yorkshire?' and Hudson likewise treats Yorkshire as uncertain.
The catalog says the manuscript was written by and for Robert Thornton of East Newton; this does not locate composition of the romance.

scope.description: Association of the Lincoln copy through its scribe and original owner

assertion.notes: This is an attributed scribal-language localization. It is kept distinct from Thornton's North Yorkshire association and from the work's tentative composition region.

scope.description: DIMEV's manuscript-level report of LALME/eLALME profile 1.98
The Lincoln manuscript witness, not authorship of the romance

notes: The XML expressly calls this a transcription and not an edition; McSparran's separate 1986 editorial role remains in lineage.

scope.description: The unpublished direct manuscript transcription represented by OctL

subjects.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion
Social rank, labor, and comic humiliation

scope.description: Florent's aristocratic character and chivalric career contrasted with Clement's bourgeois values

subjects.label: Christian-Saracen conflict and conversion
notes: This heading records the romance's hostile conventional representation of Muslims and Marsabelle's conversion; it does not endorse that representation.

summaries.value: In this Lincoln witness of the Northern Octavian romance, the Roman emperor's mother falsely accuses his empress after the birth of twin sons, causing mother and children to be exiled and separated. One son, Octavian, reaches Jerusalem with his mother and a lioness; the other, Florent, is raised by the Parisian burgess Clement, reveals his chivalric identity through combat and courtly exploits, loves the sultan's daughter Marsabelle, and is eventually reunited with his family. The witnessed text has its beginning and ending but loses substantial passages through a missing leaf after f. 102 and damage to f. 108.

OctL represents McSparran's direct transcription of the Lincoln Thornton witness, not a homogeneous critical reconstruction of Northern Octavian. The electronic text retains the manuscript's losses, while Hudson's later edition supplies missing passages from Cambridge MS Ff.2.38 and therefore has a different extent. The EETS odd-page milestones in OctL are comparison locators only.

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, using the established Octavian title while making the Lincoln witness boundary explicit

titles.value: Octovian (Lincoln, Dean, and Chapter Library, MS 91)

notes: This heading does not erase differences among the Lincoln, Cambridge, and early-print witnesses.

scope.description: Broader recension heading under which the Lincoln witness-version is cataloged

titles.supplied_by: DIMEV record 3132 and the Database of Middle English Romance

assertion.notes: The holding catalog transcribes the opening as 'Here bygynnes the Romance off Octovyane'; no target-folio image was publicly available for direct visual verification.

titles.value: Mekyll and littill olde and ȝynge / herkyns all to my talkynge

notes: Ellipsis abbreviates the six-line closing passage; it is not a supplied reconstruction.
titles.value: And thus endis Octouean ... Amen amen par charyte amen
